planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist to average, fertile to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; western white pine grows naturally in moist mountain forests of the Pacific Northwest and requires adequate moisture. Susceptible to white pine blister rust — use only certified rust-resistant stock when available.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist to average, fertile to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; western white pine (Idaho white pine) is the state tree of Idaho — a magnificent five-needle pine that once dominated vast stretches of the northern Rocky Mountains and Cascades, forming some of the most majestic forest stands in North America. The trees are tall, narrowly pyramidal when young but developing a broad, somewhat irregular crown at maturity, with long (5–8 inch), bluish-green needles in bundles of five and distinctive, elongated, curved cones 5–12 inches long. The wood was once the most important commercial timber in Idaho and the Pacific Northwest — light, straight-grained, and ideal for fine millwork, matchsticks, and patterns. White pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola), an introduced fungal disease, decimated western white pine populations throughout the 20th century. Breeding programs have produced rust-resistant varieties now used in restoration plantings. A beautiful, fast-growing ornamental for large landscape sites in cool, moist mountain climates.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; pinyon pine is highly drought tolerant once established — an essential xeriscape tree for the Southwest. Does not tolerate waterlogging, heavy clay, or excessive fertilization.

directions: Plant seeds in fall or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; pinyon pine is the most ecologically and culturally important small tree of the American Southwest — the source of the highly prized and commercially valuable piñon nuts (pine nuts) that are a cornerstone of both Native American foodways and southwestern cuisine. A two-needle pine (two needles per bundle) forming dense, round-crowned, picturesque small trees in the classic piñon-juniper woodland that dominates millions of acres of the Colorado Plateau and Great Basin at 4,500–8,000 feet elevation. Pinyon nut harvests are a major economic and cultural event across the Southwest — harvested by Native peoples and marketed commercially for centuries. The large, wingless nuts are rich in protein, fat, and calories. Drought tolerant to an extraordinary degree — essentially requiring no supplemental irrigation once established in appropriate Southwest climates. Trees live 200–1,000 years. Pinyon jays are entirely dependent on pinyon nuts as their primary food source. An excellent, slow-growing, low-water landscape tree for hot, dry, rocky gardens in zone 4–8.
sources: University of New Mexico Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to very dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; singleleaf pinyon is the most drought-tolerant of the pinyons, native to the driest Great Basin and Mojave desert transition habitats at 3,500–9,500 feet elevation.

directions: Plant seeds in fall or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to very dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; singleleaf pinyon pine is the botanical oddity of the pine family — the only pine in the world with one needle per fascicle (bundle) rather than the 2–5 needles typical of all other pines. The stout, curved, single needle is one of the most distinctive identification features in the western flora. The dominant tree of the Great Basin at mid-elevations — forming the classic Nevada and Utah basin-and-range piñon-juniper woodland with Utah juniper (Juniperus osteosperma) across millions of acres of the intermountain West. Produces large, wingless, edible pine nuts (piñon nuts) comparable in size and quality to the two-needle pinyon's nuts — both species' nuts are harvested commercially and by Native peoples. The nuts are an essential fall food for many Great Basin birds and mammals, including pinyon jays, Clark's nutcrackers, and mule deer. An exceptional xeriscape tree for hot, dry, rocky Great Basin and Mojave-edge gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky, lean, well-aerated soil in full sun; foxtail pine grows naturally at extreme high elevations on exposed, rocky, nutrient-poor, often serpentine soils in the Klamath Mountains and southern Sierra Nevada — one of the most extreme growing conditions of any tree species in North America.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky, lean, well-aerated soil in full sun; foxtail pine is a rare and distinctive five-needle pine endemic to California — growing in two widely separated populations, one in the Klamath Mountains of northwestern California and one in the high Sierra Nevada above 10,000 feet. Named for the characteristic dense clusters of needles that persist on the branchlets for 10–20 years (rather than the 2–5 years typical of most pines), creating long, bottle-brush-like branch segments resembling a fox's tail. This exceptionally long needle retention is an adaptation to the extreme high-elevation environment where growing season is very short and photosynthetic tissue is valuable. Related to and resembling the bristlecone pine (P. longaeva) in adaptation to extreme subalpine-to-treeline environments, with similar windswept, gnarled growth forms at the highest elevations. Very long-lived — ancient individuals estimated at 2,000–3,000 years exist in the Klamath populations. Rarely grown in gardens but of great interest to conifer collectors.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall or plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; Mexican pinyon is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the pinyons, native to the dry mountain ranges of northern Mexico and trans-Pecos Texas at 4,000–8,000 feet elevation.

directions: Plant seeds in fall or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; Mexican pinyon is the most widely distributed of the Mexican pinyon pines — a three-needle pinyon (three needles per bundle, distinguishing it from the two-needle Colorado pinyon) of the dry mountain ranges of Mexico and into trans-Pecos Texas, southeastern Arizona, and southern New Mexico. The most important commercial pine nut tree in Mexico and an important food plant for Indigenous peoples of northern Mexico. More heat-tolerant than Colorado pinyon, extending into warmer, drier climates of zones 6–9 in the Southwest — an excellent xeriscape tree for hot, dry Texas Hill Country, west Texas, and similar climates where Colorado pinyon is marginally hardy. The nuts are smaller than Colorado pinyon nuts but comparable in flavor and nutritional quality. Slow-growing but long-lived — old trees develop picturesque, gnarled forms on rocky, exposed slopes. An underused edible landscape tree for dry southwestern gardens.
